item.title: Licensing Junior Staff Post - Info
item.content:encoded: The image in this article has been sourced to https://www.freeimages.com/photo/alone-lamp-in-the-dark-1424161 which is non-compliant with the sites license. It has such been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is REALLY late, but I've just now seen it, so I might as well respond 8 months later than never haha

I don't know how 'secrets' work on this site, so, potential "spoilers":

The 'hidden' idea was that the Lamp only takes living things that can experience its light. Bacteria can actually sense light levels, as can flies, as can crippled/whole people. But a heart can't, nor can one who is brain dead. What is not explicitly mentioned in the experiment is that

  • the brain dead body and the heart were both devoid of the bacteria that were within them following the event
  • experimentation on viruses / prions yielded the expected results for the wrong reasons: the Foundation thinks it is because viruses / prions are not alive, while the real reason is that they cannot experience light.
  • experimentation was not thought to be done on blind individuals, but if the Foundation were to do so, they would discover that blind living things are not teleported.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand why it would leave disconnected heart or the vegetative D-class in E-3. Certainly still living organic material in both cases. If it only takes fully in tact specimens, why transport the amputee? And surely whatever causes it to leave them would make it obviously useless as a sanitizing device as the later experiment proposal suggests. I like the post quite a bit, but but the logical issues with E-3 kept me from upvoting.

item.title: Re: Greetings readers,
item.content:encoded:
If you are referring to the repeating of the 'events' definition ("henceforth…"), someone else had the same complaint; if I define the term in the containment procedures, is that fine for the whole document?

Most (all?) articles are meant to be read in order. Even if that wasn't the case, I think the awkwardness of repeating the exact same line is quite bad, while the potential to slightly confuse the reader is relatively minor.

You could also just cut the definition entirely. "SCP-2609 event" is fairly clear already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually I was considering making them an SCP of their own later on. Do you thing such a blatant connection is in bad taste? To be fair, literally the only relation between the lamp and the moon 'rocks' is how the latter got into Foundation custody; everything else would be entirely independent.

I think that would be fine. I'm fact I think it would improve this article - instead of a seemingly random log at the end, it leads the reader to another story.

Then you only need 999 more articles before you're free…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing that out, I'll clarify.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I define the 'event' term in the containment procedures, is that fine for the whole document? I figured that there will be many who start with the description, so I had it in both…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ 1
- 1

I just thought that it would simply be a logical candidate for one of the few things the Foundation is willing to destroy instead of keep trying to contain; by its nature, it will constantly be 'escaping', and each time, it has the risk of some unfortunate circumstance happening.

This may be another instance of "ok that makes sense if this were an actual report, but as a work of fiction, 'meh'".

+ 3
- 3

523's the main star

That's what I found to be amusing in a deadpan-like way. Despite all of its vast potential and un-containability, SCP-2609 is, at the end of the day, a lamp. It does not mean to escape the most secure locations on the planet every few weeks, it did not mean to drop those people off that cliff, it does not have any intentions of using its apparently unlimited spacial powers for any grand design, because it has no intentions at all; its a lamp. It won't attack, it won't infiltrate, it won't make people go crazy; it just does its thing.

So, I just liked how it just shows up and does nothing else, because, well, its a lamp. I find the whole situation to be slightly amusing (SCP-523 basically just like 'well this works out great for me' while SCP-2609 just sits there and flickers). This could just be a difference of opinion again, but if you disagree, please, discuss it further!

+ 6
- 6

The main ones I was thinking of were the rock-eggs (otherwise they just felt a bit thrown in)

Actually I was considering making them an SCP of their own later on. Do you thing such a blatant connection is in bad taste? To be fair, literally the only relation between the lamp and the moon 'rocks' is how the latter got into Foundation custody; everything else would be entirely independent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been wondering about this SCP all day, ever since I first read through it. On the first hand, it's not terribly interesting and it's pretty long (in fact, in my first read I caught myself glancing through about half the article). But on the other hand, I consider this to be an impressive feat. This is perhaps the closest we can get to a real, in-universe SCP article while still maintaining readability. It's well-thought out, and pretty much every possibility has been considered in terms of what the Foundation can do with a teleporting lamp. An article like this is a good representation of what the Foundation is: Contain the unexplainable, and scientifically deduce what it is and how it works.

And because of this, I will upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I asked Sound Chaser, do you think it would help if the order of the latter two Addendum were to be swapped?

item.title: Re: Greetings readers,
item.content:encoded:
+ The language around "events" is stilted
-  The language around "events" is stilted

I can't seem to get that right. I first had 'anomalous events', then 'SCP-XXXX events', then 'Events' as a predefined terminology, then just 'events', and every time someone complains that something sounds off. I'm not saying the complaints are invalid, just that I have not yet been able to fix this particular issue.

I suspect that this arises due to the simple number of times the term - whatever it may be - has to be used. Eventually any word, when said enough, starts to sound weird. I could be wrong about that being the cause though; do you have any suggestions about how to fix it?

+ you repeat some things between the SCP and description
-  you repeat some things between the SCP and description

If you are referring to the repeating of the 'events' definition ("henceforth…"), someone else had the same complaint; if I define the term in the containment procedures, is that fine for the whole document? I figured that there may be many who start with the description, so I had it in both…

Also, to what else do you refer?

+ I also feel like there's too much detail given.
-  I also feel like there's too much detail given.

The foundation knows almost exactly how this thing works. It may be weird, but it's completely understood.

That's actually part of what was intended to make this SCP unique; they have a really good grasp on what it does and the rules it follows, but by its nature it is still extremely unpredictable and thus can cause unforeseen incidents. Its a juxtaposition.

In addition to the (in my opinion) quaint symmetry described above, the predictability that the lamp does possess is also intended as a 'balancing' mechanic. For example:

I'd like it better if they didn't have an equation predicting precisely when it's going to jump.

If that was the case you would have an object that is completely un-containable (it will, without fail, break out in a matter of weeks every time), needs constant maintenance to even track (the live mouse must be maintained at all times), could disrupt any ongoing procedure globally at any time, and could at best cause a convicted death row inmate (D-Class used to maintain the mouse) to die a horrible painful death (if present for the now-unforeseeable events) or at worst release said convicted death row inmate into an urban area.

I actually like that it has some form of a 'weakness', so to speak, in that they know exactly when it will jump. They just have positively no clue where. That one variable is enough to make the lamp quite formidable on its own, as many of the Events demonstrated. Also, remember that another Event 2609-64 might happen, where they don't get to it in time to determine when the next jump will occur before said 'next jump' happens.

This could just be an opinion thing, though. Tell me what you think!

+ I recommend deleting words. Lots of words.
-  I recommend deleting words. Lots of words.

You're not the first to say this, and its definitely something I need to work on. Thank you!

Thank you for you time and feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In fairness, though, this seems to be moving up, so I could just be miserable and this might end up not really needing any fixing…

Nah. A positive rating doesn't mean something can't be improved. As a matter of fact, you touch on a lot of my main issues with this.

Specifically, it is rather bloated and winding in a way that's incredibly boring. There are some gems here, (I really enjoyed the first crosslink, for example) but damn, does it take too long to get to. Author, consider looking for areas to trim in the description/first collapsible. I'm sure there's some minutiae that could be easily excised or even condensed into bullet-points to improve the flow.

One thing that stuck out as incredibly strange and unnecessary:

(henceforth referred to simply as an 'event')

You have this in there twice, not sure why…also

Upon an event occurring

The way this is written reads as if you're describing something else that happens in coordination with an 'event', rather than the event itself. You could clear that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, definitely enough to upvote, but just a tiny weeny nitpick, when you said the lamp relocated to the 1969 Moon landing site, there are two of those; Apollo 11 (Sea of Tranquillity) and Apollo 12 (Ocean of Storms). Which one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Must write shorter posts
- I keep having to spend ten minutes finding the code for these things

1. Basically, I don't feel the discussion on whether to destroy it or not adds anything to the article.

2. Part of it, as Khaim says, is fatigue. The rest of it is that it doesn't really go anywhere that isn't the sort of thing you could guess from the description and effects. Put another way, it's the sort of stuff that, from an in-universe perspective, it makes sense to include, but as someone reading a work of fiction, it doesn't greatly keep me invested. Rereading it though, I do like the plane one…

3. Yeah, this is more the latter, because, at least in this case, 2609 causes the incident, but it doesn't really need to be 2609, it could have been the doctors being distracted by a fire alarm or something. Basically, it's the sort of thing that would make more sense on 523's page, because 523's the main star, and I can't help but feel like that would be the case for most incidents. I'd be interested to see any other possible ideas you had, though.

4. In all honesty, quite possibly. This goes back to what I was saying earlier; if you're going for a flat-out document style, this makes sense, don't get me wrong, I just prefer some sort of progression in the more clinical skips to keep me engaged, or something short and snappy. The order swapping isn't a bad idea, I guess. It'd probably keep me up when I started flagging.

5. No worries, it's the sort of thing that's easily rectified.

6. The main ones I was thinking of were the rock-eggs (otherwise they just felt a bit thrown in) or one or two of the potential applications. In hindsight, I'd be curious to know where the displaced matter goes, but that's not super-important.

In fairness, though, this seems to be moving up, so I could just be miserable and this might end up not really needing any fixing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
could be cut without really harming the rest of the article.

I find this fascinating, albeit in an incredulous manner. To clarify I don't mean that in a negative way, just that it is the exact opposite of what I was expecting. The 'Events' are where the concept is really fleshed out, showing a sampling of situations this "blasted lamp" tends to create. Can you explain in more detail why you feel it was not all that interesting?

Speaking only for myself: by the time I got to that log I was getting bored. The events log should be where the item is fleshed out, but by the time we get there you've given away most of the plot. Also, too many words.

item.title: Re: Greetings readers,
item.content:encoded:
It's ambitious, that's for sure. For a first try I think it's quite good. The general idea has been done before, but that's fine - you can always try to write a better version.

Style is mostly good. The language around "events" is stilted, and you repeat some things between the SCP and description. Same with the logs: you give away several facts in the description that make the corresponding log less interesting because the reader already knows what they find. And some logs are just dull: E-8 could be replaced with "the bulb reforms after each event" in the description.

I also feel like there's too much detail given. The foundation knows almost exactly how this thing works. It may be weird, but it's completely understood. I'd like it better if they didn't have an equation predicting precisely when it's going to jump.

Overall I like it, but I recommend deleting words. Lots of words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure who/what to feel sorrier for - the well-traveled mouse that died on the Moon, or the four mysterious ovular black stones who are probably wondering what on Luna happened. I like to think they're playing bridge to pass the time.

A teleporting lamp could easily have been spoopy or narmy, or even dull - this is quietly, effectively disturbing. The longer it is uncontained, the more devastation it causes - and it's 'just a lamp'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wait I just realized: if the lamp only teleports itself, living things and object inside living things, does that mean everyone who gets "abducted" by this reappear naked?

You're right, and although I was aware of this, you have brought it to my attention that I completely neglected to mention it. I've now added the relevant details to Event 2609-A, but do you think it is necessary to mention it anywhere else?

Also, taking a second read, I just realized why 523 turned into sunglasses. Good one.

I was hoping people would make that connection lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm working on this right now, and have not gotten much feedback on it. Please tell me what you think!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ I'm not so fond of the rejected neutralisation, which is largely pointless.
- I'm not so fond of the rejected neutralisation, which is largely pointless. 

Can you elaborate on what you mean here?

+ Most of the last collapsible, in fact, isn't majorly interesting in my eyes... 
- Most of the last collapsible, in fact, isn't majorly interesting in my eyes... 

… and could be cut without really harming the rest of the article.

I find this fascinating, albeit in an incredulous manner. To clarify I don't mean that in a negative way, just that it is the exact opposite of what I was expecting. The 'Events' are where the concept is really fleshed out, showing a sampling of situations this "blasted lamp" tends to create. Can you explain in more detail why you feel it was not all that interesting?

+ the 523 mention is totally pointless except just for something to happen
- the 523 mention is totally pointless except just for something to happen 

I wanted an Event to occur where SCP-2609 causes an incident with another SCP. I picked SCP-523 simply because its anomalous abilities were such that things could go very badly very fast due to even a minor distraction, much less a blinding flash of life. Do you have a suggestion for an SCP that would feel more natural, or do you simply not like the concept of SCP-2609 causing an incident?

+ ... the fact that it's the last thing there makes it worse.
-  ... the fact that it's the last thing there makes it worse.

This article is long enough to make at least me want some sort of payoff in the end, and the fact that that's all we get just leaves me with a sense of "Oh… all right then."

I think that this complaint has to do with this entry trying to 'go back to the roots' of SCPs, for lack of a better way to say what I mean. Although the many story-like, character-driven SCPs nowadays are still great in their own way, the clinical 'report' style and the relative absence of actual characters were what made the original SCPs unique and thus what made me like the old ones. There is no "payoff" in this entry because this is not a story, it is a document.

Do you think this is just a difference of opinion, or something I can work on to fix? If you think I can fix it, how?

How much do you think it would help if I simply swapped the order of the experiment and event logs? Then it is not at the end, and moreover the 'Pending Experiments' thing (which you liked) is actually a natural ending for the article, giving hints about what might happen in the future.

+ A problem I have with the actual writing is the repetition and lack of concision.
-  A problem I have with the actual writing is the repetition and lack of concision.

You're not the first to say this, and its definitely something I need to work on. Thank you!

+ perhaps ... look at one of the ideas you've brought up and put that at the focus of the article.
-  perhaps you could just look at one of the ideas you've brought up and put that at the focus of the article.

What 'ideas' do you refer to here?

Thank you for your time and feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really remember how the draft I read looked like, but I remember it just didn't grab my attention.
What made me change my mind was not only how you elaborated on how this thing is dangerous, but also how you made the Foundation study it. So many SCP forget that the Foundation's job is also to study and understand how the anomalous works. It's in the little details, like how it took 3 tries to get the equation right.

Also, taking a second read, I just realized why 523 turned into sunglasses. Good one.
EDIT: wait I just realized: if the lamp only teleports itself, living things and object inside living things, does that mean everyone who gets "abducted" by this reappear naked?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: GOI=Group of Interest. You and I have similar taste in articles, it would appear. I'm looking forward to what you bring out next!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You actually demonstrated how nasty a teleporting lamp can be. It's not a murderous entity or something that makes people insane, but it's a major pain to the Foundation.

That's exactly what I was going for! I'm glad you like the final draft.

Out of curiosity, what turned your opinion? Although I got great feedback and made many edits, the meat of the document is still more or less the same. I'm just curious what made the difference.

Thanks for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I tried to take a simple concept and fully flesh it out to its complete ramifications. I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it! This is one of the best-thought-out scips I've seen in a long time. I need to start using this article as an example of how to make an anomalous object with a relatively simple effect actually interesting. You've got great attention to detail and have explored the various possible outcomes of events (and the scip's potential uses) quite nicely.

Thanks!

And, honestly, it's refreshing to see an interesting scip with no cheesy origin story or goofy GOI keeping it afloat. It reminds me of a really good Series II article in that way.

Yes, I was going for a more 'back-to-roots' SCP, for lack of a better way to say what I mean. Although the story-like SCPs nowadays are still great in their own way, the clinical 'report' style and relative absence of actual characters were what made me like the old ones.

GOI

What does this mean in this context?

Thank you for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This shows an admirable amount of effort having been put into a very simple object, and it's a brave attempt, it just doesn't quite work for me. On the plus side, I do like that you've been thorough in exploring every avenue this thing presents, and I really like Addendum B in this regard. The sterilisation idea is particularly interesting, although I'm not so fond of the rejected neutralisation, which is largely pointless.

+ I'm having some issues with it, though
- And here they are

The main problem here is that it's… it's just too long. By the last collapsible, it really feels dragged out; nothing there is particularly notable except the rock-egg things from Event 2609-180, and that's immediately brushed under the rug. Most of the last collapsible, in fact, isn't majorly interesting in my eyes and could be cut without really harming the rest of the article.

The crosslinking has mixed success for me; the first one makes sense as an experiment, but the 523 mention is totally pointless except just for something to happen, and that something is mostly relevant to 523 anyway. I suppose, in my eyes, the fact that it's the last thing there makes it worse. This article is long enough to make at least me want some sort of payoff in the end, and the fact that that's all we get just leaves me with a sense of "Oh… all right then."

A problem I have with the actual writing is the repetition and lack of concision. Many of the sentences here feel like, if edited a bit, they could result in the page being half as long. For instance:

This can cause teleported matter to ‘overlap’ with matter already present at the new location. The teleported overlapping matter appears to take priority over matter already present at the new location, causing the already present matter to disappear where there is overlap with the teleported matter.

could easily be shortened to:

This can cause teleported matter to ‘overlap’ with matter already present at the new location, causing the former to completely replace the latter

or something like that. Admittedly, mine's just an example, but something along those lines would be a lot less tiring to read.

The simplicity of the central item isn't necessarily a bad thing, but to be honest, there isn't really enough done with it here to win my heart. Not that it's unsalvageable; perhaps you could just look at one of the ideas you've brought up and put that at the focus of the article.

Overall, I'm impressed with the level of dedication that's gone into this, and with some heavy rewriting, something along these lines could work, but as it is, I'm sorry, it's just too boring for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I can't improve it without feedback :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Teleporting lamp. I like this one a lot more than I expected to, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one seems contentious so far. I'd like to see a critique from one of the users downvoting to see what kills this article for them, as I'm a big fan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow,
I've actually seen the first few draft of this in the forums, and I must admit that back then, I was not sold at all by a teleporting lamp.

But this is great. You actually demonstrated how nasty a teleporting lamp can be. It's not a murderous entity or something that makes people insane, but it's a major pain to the Foundation. I love it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it! This is one of the best-thought-out scips I've seen in a long time. I need to start using this article as an example of how to make an anomalous object with a relatively simple effect actually interesting. You've got great attention to detail and have explored the various possible outcomes of events (and the scip's potential uses) quite nicely.
And, honestly, it's refreshing to see an interesting scip with no cheesy origin story or goofy GOI keeping it afloat. It reminds me of a really good Series II article in that way.

item.title: Image Credit
item.content:encoded:
This image is borrowed from:

Original Source: http://www.freeimages.com
Creator: Saïvann ¤

item.title: Greetings readers,
item.content:encoded:
This is my first attempt at an SCP object. Although the basic idea is quite simple, I think it still has the potential to create some interesting situations and interactions.

My style of writing is not exactly 'character-driven', so most of this is clinical descriptions of mechanics and events; I'd like to think this is in the spirit of the original SCPs, although some might find it boring in comparison to some of the more 'story-like' entries of late. I hope it is still interesting to enough people to allow my first attempt to be a success.

Thank you for your time and feedback!
